This recording was made in Rijsterbos, a 172-hectare (mostly) deciduous forest in Friesland (NL), planted in 1676 and dominated by mature oaks and beeches. It borders the IJsselmeer to the southwest and agricultural grasslands (mainly dairy farms) on other sides. The forest has varied undergrowth and offers diverse microhabitats. Human presence is moderate; visitors with dogs are common but dispersed. Occasional disturbance from nearby farm machinery along the forest edge may occur, though this is minimal.

Two song thrushes singing a couple of metres from each other (under windy conditions).
song also of turdus philomelos? Possibly 2 birds.

The bird was singing from a hedgerow between grassland fields, but quite soft and hidden in the shrubbery, not wel exposed. It was continuously mimicking, phrases of e.g. Great Tit, Marsh Tit, Common Blackbird (alarm call), Eurasian Nuthatch, Eurasian Wren (rattle), Blue Tit can be heard. 
Note that a single Blackcap song phrase is also heard, starting at 51 s. This is however, a different bird; it can be heard (and seen in the sonogram) that during the last tones of the Blackcap phrase, the mimicring bird also starts a new phrase. Apologies for the grass mowing machine...
Bird went straight over parabolic microphone!

loc: Vlakte van Waalsdorp, Meijendel, Wassenaar, Zuid-Holland
rmk:
Prolonged observation (3 recordings on XC in total) of two birds next to each other on branch of tree, in a forested area next to open dunes. The birds were sometimes briefly chasing each other. It seemed like this was a pair and they were both vocalising, but I wasn't able to confirm this visually. In any case, it's an amazing variety and range in sounds - I hadn't heard this before.

Around 06:41 one of the birds (the female?) flew down to the ground to forage on the cycle track a few meters away from me. From this moment, the bird left behind in the tree (the male?) seemed to go into full song mode.

Recorded with Edirol R09-HR (internal microphone), at about 20 meters distance from the birds.

loc: Vlakte van Waalsdorp, Meijendel, Wassenaar, Zuid-Holland
rmk:
Prolonged observation (3 recordings on XC in total) of two birds next to each other on branch of tree, in a forested area next to open dunes. The birds were sometimes briefly chasing each other. It seemed like this was a pair and they were both vocalising, but I wasn't able to confirm this visually. In any case, it's an amazing variety and range in sounds - I hadn't heard this before.

There's also a singing Song Thrush in the background of this recording - this was a third bird further away from the ones I was observing and recording.

Recorded with Edirol R09-HR (internal microphone), at about 20 meters distance from the birds.

type: call, flight call, nocturnal flight call, aberrant
rmk: Strange double call at 0:01 of which the first one is normal but the second is aberrant (lower and quite complex for song trush). One normal call again at 0:18. Maybe two separate calls instead of a double call (similar to this XC513320 recording) but a double call is perhaps more likely (like the calls at 1s and 15s of this XC680153 recording).

This mystery recording was solved by Georg Amann as being nocturnal flight calls of a song thrush, thank you!
More nocturnal flight calls of song thrushes can be found here on Xeno Canto: <https://xeno-canto.org/set/5025>


I heard some calls during the night. It sounded as if the animal was flying over the area, due to the difference in loudness and direction of the calls. There was one moment when a call was right above my head. I went home to retrieve recording equipment, since I wanted to figure out what I heard. The calls aready started to get less regulary emitted when I returned at the location, but I was able to record these, altough it sounded like they were emitted from a little distance. I went back home once again to retrieve a thermal camera. Sadly the calls had stopped the moment I returned to the location once more.

It was near a small city park containing multiple trees and a pond, surrounded by buildings.
Night recording to gather practice material for myself.
